Barclays vs Peers: Data‑Led Narrative for Consulting Leadership

Purpose: Ground a leadership conversation in observable data, establish where Barclays sits versus peers, and create structured space to brainstorm where and how intervention could move outcomes. Tone: Neutral, analytical, non‑commercial.

Slide 1 — Why this conversation

  • Barclays has delivered a material improvement in performance versus peers.
  • That improvement is not yet fully reflected in relative valuation.
  • The gap between performance and belief creates a fact‑based discussion space.

This is no longer a turnaround discussion. It is a question of durability, confidence, and sustainability of outcomes.

Slide 2 — Barclays vs peers: headline performance

  • Barclays now sits mid‑to‑upper tier on recent shareholder returns and profitability improvement.
  • Performance momentum is clearly visible in the data.
MetricBarclays PLCPeer Context
5‑Year TRS CAGR27.6%Comparable with top European / global peers
3‑Year TRS CAGR42.1%Upper end of peer set
Pre‑Tax ROE (FY‑12/2025)12.5%Improving into low‑teens

Implication: Delivery credibility has been re‑established.

Slide 3 — The valuation tension

  • Despite improved returns, Barclays trades below book value.
  • This contrasts with several peers that command clear equity premiums.
Metric (Feb 2026)Barclays PLCSelected Peers (Illustrative)
P/BV (Equity Premium)~0.97x>1.0x for many US and select European peers
P/E Multiple~8.1xHigher for stronger confidence franchises

Implication: Markets are signalling caution on sustainability and predictability, not current effort.

Slide 4 — Where the belief gap shows up

  1. Efficiency: Cost ratios improving, but still structurally higher than best‑in‑class.
  2. Asset & capital productivity: ROE improving faster than ROA.
  3. Earnings mix perception: Heavy IB contribution and consumer credit risk create volatility optics.
IndicatorTrendWhat it Signals
Cost / Income RatioImproving, ~60%Progress, but not structural leadership
ROA0.5% → 0.6%Asset efficiency remains modest
IB Income Share~45%Cyclicality perception

Slide 5 — Reframing the conversation: outcomes, not initiatives

  • Which outcome metrics matter most to closing the belief gap?
  • Which levers move those metrics in a durable way?

This is not about programmes or methodologies. It is about shifting observable outcomes that markets respond to.

Slide 6 — Outcome pathway 1: Cost & productivity credibility

  • Cost ratios are trending down.
  • Volatility remains in non‑staff and “other” cost categories.
Outcome ChainDescription
Doing XStabilising cost drivers, simplifying execution, improving run/change economics
Moves YCost predictability, productivity per FTE, margin resilience
Results in ZHigher confidence in earnings sustainability

Brainstorming prompt: Where do current operating choices introduce cost volatility?

Slide 7 — Outcome pathway 2: Capital & asset productivity

  • ROE improvement outpaces ROA improvement.
  • Returns increasingly driven by mix and margin rather than asset efficiency.
Outcome ChainDescription
Doing XImproved capital allocation discipline and asset utilisation
Moves YROA, RoRWA, capital efficiency
Results in ZSustainable ROE without increased risk perception

Brainstorming prompt: Where is capital absorbed without proportional return?

Slide 8 — Outcome pathway 3: Earnings volatility perception

  • Strong IB contribution improves returns but increases cyclicality optics.
  • Consumer credit profitability comes with elevated loss ratios.
Outcome ChainDescription
Doing XImproving predictability, transparency, and control of earnings drivers
Moves YForecast accuracy, earnings variance
Results in ZReduced valuation discount driven by volatility concerns

Brainstorming prompt: Where does perceived volatility exceed actual volatility?

Slide 9 — What this opens up for discussion

  • The data points to execution strength, not strategic weakness.
  • The belief gap is driven by durability, predictability, and mix.
  • Opportunities sit at the intersection of operating model, cost, capital, and technology.

Discussion focus: Where could targeted intervention most credibly shift the outcomes the market responds to?

Slide 10 — How to use this pack

  • Use the narrative slides to align on the story.
  • Use the tables as anchors for evidence‑based brainstorming.
  • Stay focused on outcomes, not solutions.

This material is intended to provoke informed discussion, not prescribe action.

Appendix — Peer Data Tables (Reference for Brainstorming)

Appendix A — Shareholder Returns & Valuation

MetricBarclays PLCPeer MedianPeer Range (Indicative)
5‑Year TRS CAGR27.6%Low‑20s %Mid‑teens → High‑30s
3‑Year TRS CAGR42.1%Mid‑30s %High‑20s → High‑40s
P/BV (Feb 2026)~0.97x~1.1x~0.7x → >1.5x
P/E (LTM)~8.1xLow‑teensSingle‑digit → High‑teens

Appendix B — Profitability & Efficiency

MetricBarclays PLCPeer MedianWhat to Note
Pre‑Tax ROE12.5%Low‑teensRecovered into competitive range
ROA~0.6%~0.8%Below asset‑efficient leaders
Cost / Income Ratio~60%Mid‑50s %Improving, not best‑in‑class
Staff Cost / Income~36–37%Low‑30s %Structural productivity question

Appendix C — Capital & Risk

MetricBarclays PLCPeer ContextWhat to Note
CET1 Ratio~14.3%ComparableCapital not a binding constraint
Total Capital Ratio~20%ComparableBalance sheet resilience
RoRWAImprovingMixedQuality of returns improving

Appendix D — Business Mix & Volatility Drivers

DimensionBarclays PLCPeer ContextImplication
Investment Bank Income Share~45%Higher than many EU peersCyclicality perception
UK Retail / Corporate~37% combinedCore anchorCredit sensitivity
US Consumer~13%Higher loss ratiosRisk optics despite improving margins

Appendix E — Productivity & Scale

MetricBarclays PLCPeer ContextWhat to Note
Employees~93kLarge‑scale universal bankScale complexity
Operating Income / Employee~£313kBelow top quartileExecution efficiency lever
EBT / Employee~£101kImprovingProfitability per head recovering

Appendix tables are intended as directional, discussion‑supporting references rather than precise investor disclosures.
